turns.content: I'm new to this project and need to understand how we parse binary objects. Is it just a simple string split?

That's not quite right; parsing object files requires understanding the specific format like ELF or Mach-O, which defines where sections start and how symbols are indexed. You cannot treat them as plain text strings because they contain structural data that must be interpreted according to their header layout.
I see. So we load the whole file into memory first?
Exactly, you read the entire binary into a Vec<u8> or similar buffer type so that your parser can walk through the headers and section tables sequentially without needing random access from disk.
Got it. But what if we don't load everything at once? Can we use something like an iterator over bytes?

You could, but for most object parsers, loading the full buffer first is simpler because you need to know where each section begins relative to others. The crate usually provides traits like IntoIterator or specific Reader implementations that wrap your Vec<u8> so you can iterate through the file structure safely.
That makes sense. But what about symbols? Are they just a list of strings?

No, symbols in object files are structured entries with names, types, and binding attributes that follow the format specification. You must parse each symbol's offset and size correctly to understand how it relates to other parts of the binary.
Right. So when I write my own parser, do I just assume every file is ELF?

No, you must check the magic number at the start of the buffer to determine if it's ELF, Mach-O, or COFF, then switch your parsing logic accordingly. The crate likely has a trait like ParseObject that handles this dispatch internally so you don't have to write separate parsers for each format manually.

I'm trying to understand why Rust crates for binary parsing exist and how they handle the raw bytes, but it feels like I'm just dumping data into a format that doesn't match my mental model of what an object actually is. Why does this even work this way?

It's totally understandable to feel confused when you first encounter something like the ELF or Mach-O formats because they look like magic numbers compared to how we usually structure code in Rust. The key insight here is that these crates are essentially acting as translators between those binary layouts and high-level Rust types, so instead of manually mapping every byte offset, you can just let them handle the parsing for you using traits like IntoIterator or specific deserialization functions tailored to the format.
So I should be reading raw bytes directly without any intermediate steps? That sounds inefficient compared to what I've seen in other languages where everything is pre-processed.

Actually, no one is really doing that anymore because it's just too error-prone and slow. Modern Rust crates like obj or elf-reader do the heavy lifting by using safe abstractions so you don't have to deal with manual memory management or offset calculations yourself. They wrap those raw bytes into structured types like ObjectFile or SectionInfo, which makes your code much cleaner.
But wait, I thought Rust was all about zero-copy operations and avoiding allocations, but these crates seem to be allocating a lot of intermediate structures for every symbol read?

You're right that the goal is efficiency, but those crates are doing something clever by using smart allocation strategies like pooling or lazy initialization so you only allocate when you actually need specific sections rather than loading everything upfront. Instead of making your program crash with a segfault from bad pointers, they use safe abstractions to guarantee correctness while still keeping performance high.

I get it now about the safety, but I'm still stuck on how these crates handle the different architectures like x86_64 versus ARM without me having to manually check every single byte pattern. It feels like there's too much hidden complexity here.
Wait, does that mean I can't write my own parser if I want more control? Or is it just better to use these crates?

You absolutely can write your own parser if you really need granular control over every detail of the binary layout, but for most applications using established crates like obj or elf-reader is way safer and faster because they've already solved all those edge cases around different architectures and endianness issues. However, if you do want to implement something custom, Rust makes it easy by letting you define your own traits and derive implementations.
Okay so the crates are basically just providing a standard interface that everyone uses? That's why they're so popular then?

Exactly! The community has converged on these specific crates because they provide consistent interfaces for parsing ELF, COFF, and Mach-O files without needing to worry about low-level details. Instead of reinventing the wheel every time you want to read a symbol table or section header, you just import the crate and call their methods.
But what if I need to parse something that isn't one of these standard formats? Can I still use Rust for that?

You can absolutely! If you're dealing with a custom binary format or a newer architecture like RISC-V, you don't have to wait for a new crate. You can just write your own parser in Rust using the same pattern of wrapping raw bytes into safe types and implementing traits for iteration or deserialization.
So I should stop worrying about whether my code is idiomatic and just focus on writing parsers that are correct first?

That's a great mindset shift! While correctness is the foundation, idiomatic Rust also means using standard crates when available because they follow community conventions. Instead of reinventing something proven to work well, you can leverage existing tools like obj or elf-reader which handle complex cases automatically so your code stays readable and maintainable.

One last thing I'm confused about is how these crates deal with the different symbol types across platforms without me having to manually parse each one separately. It feels like there's magic happening in the background.

You're noticing exactly what makes Rust great here! Those crates use polymorphic traits and generic type parameters so they can handle symbols from any platform uniformly under a single interface. Instead of writing separate parsing logic for every architecture, you just implement one trait once and it works everywhere because the crate handles the underlying format differences internally.

I'm trying to build a tool that parses ELF headers, but I keep getting confused about the difference between an object file and a full binary. Specifically, what is the actual difference between an .o file and a regular executable in terms of how they are structured?

The core distinction lies in their section layout and linking requirements. An object file typically contains only unlinked code and data sections like `.text` or `.data`, whereas an executable is a fully linked binary with all dependencies resolved, usually containing the `INTERP` program header required by the loader. When you read symbols from an object file using crates like `binwalk` or `elf_reader`, you'll notice they lack the dynamic relocations found in executables.

That makes sense regarding sections, but I'm still stuck on how to actually parse those raw bytes. If I read the file header as a `Vec<u8>`, what specific Rust types or traits do I need to implement to correctly extract the machine type and entry point without writing my own low-level parsing logic?

You don't necessarily need to write raw parsing logic from scratch if you leverage crates like `binwalk` which provides a high-level abstraction over ELF structures. These crates expose traits like `Parseable` that allow you to deserialize the binary stream into typed Rust structs representing the ELF header, sections, and program headers without manually handling byte offsets or endianness conversions.

I tried using `binwalk`, but my code is crashing when I try to access the symbol table. It seems like the crate isn't automatically resolving the section index mapping for me. How do I manually map the raw section headers to the actual string tables and names so I can iterate over them safely?

The issue is likely that `binwalk` requires you to explicitly handle the relationship between section indices and their corresponding offsets. You need to read the section header table, where each entry contains an index into the string tables for names and offsets for data. Once you have the raw section headers as a slice of your custom struct type, you can iterate through them and use the `offset` field combined with the `string_table_offset` from the ELF header to construct full section names.

Okay, I see. But what if the object file is stripped? Then I can't even find the string table offsets. Is there a way to handle this gracefully in my Rust code using traits like `Option` or by checking specific ELF flags?

Yes, you should check the ELF header's `e_shstr` field which points to the section header string table. If an object file is stripped, this pointer will be null or invalid. In your Rust code using `binwalk`, you can wrap the parsing logic in a `Result` type and use `Option<T>` for fields that might be missing. You can also inspect the ELF flags field (`e_shstr`) directly; if it indicates stripping, simply skip the string table lookup step.

I'm still struggling with how to handle endianness when reading the raw bytes. If I load the file into a `Vec<u8>`, does Rust automatically handle big-endian vs little-endian conversion for the ELF header fields, or do I need to manually swap them?

Rust's standard library doesn't auto-handle endianness for raw byte vectors. You must explicitly specify the endianness when constructing your Rust structs from the binary data. When you parse the ELF header using a crate like `binwalk`, you typically provide an explicit endianness parameter (e.g., `Endian::Little` or `Endian::Big`) to ensure all multi-byte fields in your custom structs are interpreted correctly.

That clarifies things, but I'm still confused about how the symbol table is actually laid out. If I read the raw bytes for the symbol names, how do I know which byte sequence corresponds to a specific function name without knowing the exact format of the string table entries?

The symbol table in an ELF object file is structured as a series of `Elf_Sym` structs, where each entry contains a `st_name` field pointing to the start of its string data. The actual names are stored contiguously after the section header string table ends. You can iterate over your parsed `Elf_Shdr` slice, read the `st_name` offset for each symbol, and then use that offset to access the corresponding string in the combined string table array.

This is getting complicated. Can you give me a concrete example of how I would declare my custom Rust struct type for an ELF header using `binwalk` so that I don't have to manually define all the fields?

You can use `binwalk::elf::Elf64` or `Elf32` directly as your custom struct type. These types are already defined by the crate and implement traits like `Read` for parsing binary files. You don't need to manually declare fields; instead, you instantiate one of these structs (e.g., `let header = Elf64::parse(&file)?`) and access its properties via dot notation, which handles all the underlying byte-level complexity.

I've been working with Python's ast module and Perl regex for years, so I'm used to loading external libraries just to get my hands on the data. But when it comes to Rust crates like this one, I don't see a `load_object_file` function anywhere in the docs. Is there actually a crate that parses raw object files or binary headers?

You're looking for something similar to how you might use a standard library crate, but for Rust's ecosystem, we typically rely on crates like elf and rust-elf-parser which handle parsing ELF/COFF/Mach-O headers and sections. These crates expose traits like Read or specific struct types representing symbol tables so you can iterate over them without writing your own low-level parser from scratch.

That sounds convenient, but I'm still worried about the overhead of loading a whole crate just to read a single object file. I don't want to import `serde_json::Value` and all those dependencies if I only need to parse a few bytes. Is there a lightweight way to do this without pulling in heavy dependencies?

You're right to be concerned about the overhead, but these crates are designed with modularity in mind so you can use specific traits or read-only APIs instead of loading everything at once. The key is understanding how object files store sections and symbols; by leveraging the parser's internal logic, you can extract just the data you need without unnecessary bloat.
So I should be able to write my own parser if I want to avoid dependencies? But then why do we have these crates if I can just read the raw bytes myself?

You're touching on a crucial distinction: while you could parse manually, crates like elf handle edge cases and complex formats that are error-prone to implement correctly. They provide traits and helper functions so you don't have to reinvent the wheel, but they also document exactly how these parsers work so you can understand the underlying mechanics if you need to optimize or extend them.
